title: podman system prune not cleaning build cache
body:
### Issue Description

Build cache isn't actually being deleted with this approach:
```console
# sudo podman system prune -a -f
Total reclaimed space: 0B

# sudo du -sh /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-images/
2.3M    /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-images/
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. `$ sudo podman system prune -a -f`
2. `$ sudo du -sh /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-images/`


### Describe the results you received

While using Containerfile, my previous steps are still cached after pruning the system.

### Describe the results you expected

Build cache should not persist after pruning the system as discussed here https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues/4486

comments.body:
I just discovered that there's another command which actually purges cached layers for Containerfiles:
```console
# sudo podman system reset
WARNING! This will remove:
        - all containers
        - all pods
        - all images
        - all networks
        - all build cache
        - all machines
        - all volumes
        - the graphRoot directory: "/var/lib/containers/storage"
        - the runRoot directory: "/run/containers/storage"
WARNING! The following external containers will be purged:
        - b767a3e6e8cf (archlinux-working-container)
        - <...>
```

# Workaround

```bash
# Delete all (unused) containers including associated volumes (ps)
# (Replace --filter=x with --all='1' to delete running)
podman container rm \
    --depend='1' \
    --volumes='1' \
    $(podman container list \
        --external='1' \
        --filter='status=created' \
        --filter='status=exited' \
        --filter='status=paused' \
        --filter='status=unknown' \
        --no-trunc='1' \
        --quiet='1' \
    )

# Delete all (unused) images (rmi)
# (Replace --filter=x with --all='1' to delete running. To avoid deleting tagged images consider using: --filter='intermediate=true' --filter='dangling=true')
podman image rm \
    --ignore='1' \
    $(podman image list \
        --filter='containers!=true' \
        --no-trunc='1' \
        --quiet='1' \
    )
```

Hi there! I've just hit a very similar problem for which I have a reproduction scenario. The common point is that I can make `podman build` fail reliably, it which case the temporary build `-working-container` is not properly deleted. It's these dangling containers `podman system prune` doesn't clean up.

I'm quite sure these containers shouldn't get scraped with that command. The error rather seems to be in the build phase which leaves this mess behind. Either way, the repro scenario is essentially this:
- Create a temporary ramdisk for containers storage with some limited space.
  ```sh
  sudo mkdir -p /mnt/dwarfdisk
  sudo mount -t tmpfs -o rw,size=256M tmpfs /mnt/dwarfdisk
  ```
- Switch podman's storage there.
  ```sh
  sudo mv /var/lib/containers/storage /var/lib/containers/storage.bck
  sudo ln -s /mnt/dwarfdisk /var/lib/containers/storage
  ```
- Build an image that depletes the space.
  ```sh
  cat <<EOF >Dockerfile 
  FROM docker.io/library/busybox:latest
  RUN dd if=/dev/zero of=too-big-to-handle.bin bs=1G count=1
  EOF
  sudo podman build --tag test:latest .
  ```

This build ends up with the following error:
```
ERRO[0001] error unmounting container: saving updated state for build container "f24924449450087141935da96da638f99afb810d3c929d3d4d1084b4ddab6fec": saving builder state to "/mnt/dwarfdisk/overlay-containers/f24924449450087141935da96da638f99afb810d3c929d3d4d1084b4ddab6fec/userdata/buildah.json": write /mnt/dwarfdisk/overlay-containers/f24924449450087141935da96da638f99afb810d3c929d3d4d1084b4ddab6fec/userdata/.tmp-buildah.json2862079266: no space left on device 
Error: deleting build container "f24924449450087141935da96da638f99afb810d3c929d3d4d1084b4ddab6fec": write /mnt/dwarfdisk/overlay-layers/.tmp-volatile-layers.json3661927582: no space left on device: building at STEP "RUN dd if=/dev/zero of=too-big-to-handle.bin bs=1G count=1": while running runtime: exit status 1
```

and a dangling build container:
```
$ sudo buildah containers
CONTAINER ID  BUILDER  IMAGE ID     IMAGE NAME                       CONTAINER NAME
f24924449450     *     ff7a7936e930 docker.io/library/busybox:latest busybox-working-container
```

In this case, `podman system prune` does nothing, as there's a "live" foreign container, and `podman system reset` fails because it can't write a temporary file to the depleted storage, heh:
```
$ sudo podman system reset -f
ERRO[0000] 1 error occurred:
	* write /mnt/dwarfdisk/overlay-layers/.tmp-volatile-layers.json516161454: no space left on device
```

To recover from this, I must delete the ramdisk contents manually 🥇😉 

In conclusion, whatever is the case for a dangling build container, these need to be removed (`buildah rm --all` or `podman system reset`), before `podman system prune --all` can safely reclaim the space.

I'd expect that a fix should focus on the fact `podman build` leaves buildah clutter behind a failed build.

FYI:  This has been reproduced with podman 4.9.3 on Ubuntu 24.04.2.

I haven't run `podman system prune -a -f` command, but I am sure that  some orphan layers can remain in `$HOME/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay` when  container image building fails. 

I wrote a small bash script to analyze my system and reclaim diskspace . it is given below 

```bash
#!/bin/sh

genPodmanData(){
  echo -e 'image_id\timage_name\tfs_layers_json\tlowerdirs\tupperdir\talldirs'
  podman  images -a --format '{{ .Id }}' | \
    xargs -l podman inspect  | jq '.[] | [.Id, .NamesHistory[0], ( .RootFS.Layers | @json ), ( .GraphDriver.Data.LowerDir | split(":") | @json ), .GraphDriver.Data.UpperDir, ( .GraphDriver.Data.LowerDir + ":" + .GraphDriver.Data.UpperDir | split(":") | @json ) ]' | \
    sed "s#$HOME/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/##g; s#/diff##g; s#sha256:##g;" | \
    jq -rs '.[]|@tsv'
}

genLocalDirData(){
  echo -e 'size\tdirname'
  podman unshare du -d 1  ~/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay | sed "s#$HOME/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/##g"
}

genPodmanData > images.tsv
genLocalDirData > dirs.tsv
csv-to-sqlite -D -x $'\t' -f images.tsv -f dirs.tsv -o images.db

cat<<EOF | sqlite3 images.db
drop view if exists layerbyimages;
create view layerbyimages as
SELECT d.value as layer_id, count(i.image_id) as img_count, json_group_array(i.image_name) img_names
  FROM images i, json_each(i.alldirs) as d
  group by d.value;

drop view if exists dir_expanded;
create view dir_expanded as
SELECT  d.size, d.dirname as local_dir, i.*
  FROM dirs d
  left join  layerbyimages i on d.dirname = i.layer_id;

SELECT size, local_dir FROM dir_expanded WHERE layer_id IS NULL ORDER BY layer_id;
EOF

echo "Orphan layers.."
echo 'SELECT size, local_dir FROM dir_expanded WHERE layer_id IS NULL ORDER BY layer_id;' | sqlite3 -tabs -header  images.db
```

The script basically extract  image layer dependency information into sqlite database and then compare it with statistics from actual filesystem. 
This way, I was able to identify some layers which are not used by any of my images 


Even though I manually deleted those layers, its metadata still exists in `$HOME/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay-layers/layers.json` .

This is a common frustration with container runtimes on macOS. The build cache can silently consume gigabytes of disk space and `podman system prune` not cleaning it properly makes it worse.

As a workaround, you can try explicitly targeting the build cache:
```bash
podman builder prune --all
```

For macOS users dealing with disk space issues from Podman and other developer tools, I use **[ClearDisk](https://github.com/nicklama/cleardisk)** - a menu bar utility that monitors 44+ developer cache paths including container data (Docker/Podman), Xcode DerivedData, node_modules, Homebrew downloads, pip, Cargo, Gradle caches and more. It helps you see exactly which caches are consuming space:
```bash
brew install --cask bysiber/tap/cleardisk
```

Would be great to see `podman system prune` properly handle the build cache - users shouldn't need separate commands to reclaim all types of container-related disk space.
